response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research: its precursor, ARPANET, was first demonstrated in 1969 via a message from UCLA to the Stanford Research Institute, 1983’s transition to TCP/IP protocols formed the modern internet’s technical foundation, and Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 CERN invention of the World Wide Web (with HTML, URLs, and HTTP) plus 1993’s Mosaic browser opened the web to ordinary, non-academic users. Key post-1990s developments included the late-1990s dot-com investment boom (followed by a 2001 crash that spared future corporate giants Amazon and Google), 2006’s launch of AWS (pioneering cloud computing), the mid-2000s rise of social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube) that redefined global communication, and 2007’s iPhone launch spurring ubiquitous mobile internet access. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people worldwide, underpinning nearly every aspect of modern life from commerce and communication to entertainment.
